headline: Chip stocks start Q3 with a dud after record Q2 rallies
detail: After a massive second-quarter run, semiconductors are seeing profit-taking right out of the gate in Q3. This directly pressures SMH, SOXL, and by extension QQQ and QQQI. On margin, even a moderate sector pullback compounds your existing losses in SOXL and SPAL.
rec: No action beyond honoring existing stops. SMH at $620 with a $540 stop has room, but SOXL's $200 stop needs active monitoring today given the sector tone - not a moment to add.

headline: Meta pops 9% on cloud compute pivot
detail: Meta surging on selling excess AI compute capacity is a double-edged signal for your book - it's a positive for broad tech sentiment but could signal rotation away from pure semiconductor hardware plays toward software/cloud names. That could keep pressure on SMH and SOXL.
rec: No direct action - you don't hold Meta. Watch whether this lifts QQQ (you hold, +1.47%) or whether semi names stay weak despite the broad tech pop; that divergence tells you a lot about where money is flowing.

headline: PANW scanner setup - grade A, 9/12
detail: PANW shows the strongest mechanical setup on the scanner today - daily, 4H, and 1H all trending up, price pulling into a 1H order block. Cybersecurity has been resilient even in choppy tape. CRWD is your existing cyber name and is holding well (+7.4%).
rec: PANW entry zone $351.34-$351.69, stop $350.62 per scanner. Given you already have CRWD as your cyber exposure and your leveraged positions need capital/attention first, this is a watchlist idea only - not a priority add today.

headline: Dan Ives exits Wedbush for new venture
detail: Ives is one of the most widely followed tech bulls on the Street - his departure from Wedbush could temporarily reduce high-profile bullish coverage of names like MSFT, AAPL, and broad tech. It's a soft sentiment factor, not a fundamental shift.
rec: No trade warranted - this is a personnel change, not a market event. Your holdings are unaffected in any fundamental way; ignore this one.
